18.text: Sampling does not change the model's knowledge at all, only how we turn its logits into decisions, so the surrounding setup stays intentionally boring.

19.text: Same corpus, same trained model family. The lab is isolating one question: how much output behavior comes from decoding policy alone?

20.text: Reproducibility matters here because sampling is deliberately stochastic and we want to compare strategies, not chase random drift.

22.text: PyTorch carries the same model as earlier labs. The interesting action comes after the model emits logits, not inside the network itself.

23.text: The transformer stays the same. That makes the lesson sharper: wildly different outputs can come from changing only the decoding rule.

24.text: Softmax is central here because every sampling strategy is really a different way of shaping or pruning the probability distribution before selection.

26.text: Lock both RNGs so the demonstrations are repeatable and the difference between strategies is interpretable.

27.text: PyTorch's RNG also controls sampling itself, so seeding it is part of making the comparison honest.

32.text: Construct a portable file path to the shared dataset, relative to this script's location, so the code works regardless of where you clone the repo.

33.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

39.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

40.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

42.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

43.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

44.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

50.text: Same model config as lab 03, the model is identical, only the sampling changes.

51.text: Four parallel attention heads, each processing a different 4-dimensional slice of the embedding.

52.text: A single transformer layer is enough for this small character-level model.

53.text: Maximum context window of 16 tokens, which comfortably fits the longest names in the dataset.

54.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the 16-dimensional embedding.

57.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

63.text: Divide by the root-mean-square of the activation vector. Simpler than LayerNorm since it skips mean subtraction, but equally effective for stabilizing training.

66.text: Standard causal self-attention, identical to lab 03.

70.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

71.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

72.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

74.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

75.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

76.text: Project and reshape queries into heads.

77.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

78.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

80.text: Compute scaled dot-product attention scores between every pair of query and key vectors.

81.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

82.text: Replace future-position scores with negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight.

83.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

85.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape back to the original embedding layout.

86.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

89.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

92.text: Project each token's embedding into a 4x wider hidden space for richer computation.

93.text: Compress the expanded hidden representation back down to the original embedding dimension.

95.text: Run the input through the two-layer MLP with ReLU activation in between.

96.text: Two linear layers with ReLU in between. The expansion to 4x width gives the network more room to learn nonlinear transformations before compressing back down.

99.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

102.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

103.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

104.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

105.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

107.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

108.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

109.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

113.text: The full transformer model, identical to lab 03, serving as the shared backbone for all sampling strategies.

116.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

117.text: Learned positional embeddings that tell the model where each token sits in the sequence.

118.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

119.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

120.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

121.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

125.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

128.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

129.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

130.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

131.text: A learned vector for each position in the sequence. Added to token embeddings so the model knows where each token sits, since attention alone is position-agnostic.

132.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

133.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

135.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

141.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

142.text: Instantiate the model with the same architecture as lab 03. The same trained weights will be used by every sampling strategy, isolating the effect of the sampler.

143.text: Display the total parameter count to verify the model size before training.

145.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

146.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this tiny model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

148.text: Standard training loop, identical to lab 03.

149.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

150.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

151.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits inside the model's positional embedding table.

153.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

154.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

156.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

157.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

159.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

160.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

161.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

162.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

164.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

174.text: The simplest possible strategy: always pick the most likely token. Deterministic, produces the same output every time. Safe but boring.

176.text: argmax = highest probability token. No randomness at all.

179.text: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution. Low temperature sharpens it (confident), high temperature flattens it (random). The model weights don't change, you're just adjusting the "confidence knob."

183.text: Divide logits by T before softmax. T=0.5 doubles the logits, making the peak sharper. T=2.0 halves them, spreading probability more evenly.

187.text: Top-k: hard cutoff at exactly k candidates. Simple and effective, but the cutoff is arbitrary, sometimes 5 tokens are plausible, sometimes 50 are.

190.text: Scale the logits by temperature first to reshape the distribution, then mask everything outside the top k. This order matters because temperature changes which tokens are "top."

191.text: Find the k-th highest logit value.

192.text: Everything below the threshold becomes -infinity. Exactly k tokens survive.

193.text: Softmax over only the surviving tokens, they now get all the probability mass.

197.text: Top-p (nucleus sampling): keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Adapts to the distribution, peaked distributions get few candidates, flat ones get many.

201.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities before sorting. The temperature reshapes the distribution first, which affects which tokens fall inside the nucleus.

202.text: Sort tokens by probability, highest first.

203.text: Cumulative sum: after each token, how much total probability have we accumulated?

205.text: Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds p, but always keep the top token. The subtraction ensures the first token that pushes past p is kept.

206.text: Zero out tokens beyond the nucleus.

209.text: Scatter back to original token ordering for sampling.

210.text: Put filtered probabilities back at their original indices.

211.text: Draw one token randomly from the nucleus. Only the tokens inside the cumulative probability threshold compete, so unlikely tokens are excluded naturally.

214.text: Min-p: keep tokens with probability >= min_p * max_prob. Simpler than top-p, naturally adapts: when the model is confident (high max_prob), threshold is high and few tokens pass; when uncertain, threshold drops and more tokens are considered.

218.text: Divide logits by temperature and convert to probabilities. The temperature is applied before min-p filtering to reshape which tokens clear the relative threshold.

219.text: Threshold = min_p fraction of the highest probability. A relative cutoff, not absolute.

220.text: Zero out anything below the threshold.

221.text: Divide by the new total so the surviving probabilities sum back to one after filtering.

222.text: Draw one token from the tokens that survived the min-p filter. Fewer survivors when the model is confident, more when it is uncertain, all handled automatically.

230.text: Shared generation function: same model, different sampler, that's the whole point of this lab.

232.text: Allocate an empty list to collect the generated names. Each strategy fills this list, and we display them side-by-side afterward for easy comparison.

234.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

236.text: Feed current sequence through the model.

237.text: Extract the raw logit vector for the last position, which predicts what token comes next.

238.text: Extract the raw logits for the last position, the model's prediction for what comes next.

239.text: Clone so each sampler gets a fresh copy (some modify logits in-place).

240.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

242.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and move on to the next position.

243.text: Convert the list of generated token IDs back into characters and join them into a readable name string.

255.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs without training noise.

256.text: Disable gradient tracking since we only need forward passes for generation, not training.

257.text: Ten sampling strategies, all using the exact same trained model.

258.text: Greedy: deterministic, always the most likely name. Same output every time.

259.text: T=0.3: nearly greedy but with a tiny bit of randomness. Very conservative names.

260.text: T=1.0: the model's own learned distribution, unmodified. The "honest" output.

261.text: T=2.0: chaotic, flattens probabilities so rare tokens get sampled often. Produces gibberish.

262.text: Top-k=3 with T=0.8: only the 3 most likely tokens compete. Simple but rigid.

263.text: Top-k=10 with T=0.8: 10 candidates. More variety than k=3.

264.text: Top-p=0.5 with T=0.8: tight nucleus, keeps just enough tokens to cover 50% of probability mass.

265.text: Top-p=0.95 with T=0.8: loose nucleus, almost everything gets a chance.

266.text: Min-p=0.1 with T=0.8: keep tokens with >= 10% of the top token's probability. Adapts automatically.

267.text: Min-p=0.01 with T=0.8: very permissive, most tokens pass the threshold.

270.text: Loop through all ten sampling configurations, generate 20 names with each, and print the results. Same model throughout, so any differences come purely from the sampler.

272.text: Reset RNG for each strategy so they all start from the same random state.

286.text: Visualize how each strategy reshapes the probability distribution for a specific context.

287.text: Encode "mar" to see what the model predicts next, likely 'i', 'y', 'a', etc.

291.text: Run the model on "mar" to get raw logits for the next token.

292.text: Logits for the next token after "mar".

295.text: Helper to format the top probabilities as a readable string.

296.text: Show the top tokens and their probabilities, plus how many tokens have non-zero probability.

308.text: Raw model distribution at T=1.0, the model's unfiltered opinion.

311.text: T=0.3: the distribution becomes very peaked, the top token dominates.

315.text: T=2.0: the distribution flattens out, even unlikely tokens get noticeable probability.

319.text: Top-k=3: exactly 3 tokens survive, rest are zero. Hard cutoff.

326.text: Top-p=0.9: keep tokens until cumulative probability hits 90%. Variable number of candidates.

337.text: Min-p=0.1: keep tokens with >= 10% of the max probability. Naturally adaptive.
